title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: model uses odelia assembled four files:

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: system class templated scalar type T (code works double AD number type). must implement small contract: Templating T makes system AD-ready — see parameter-fitting vignette.
code: template <typename T = double> class LeafThermalSystem { public:   // number of state variables   size_t ode_size() const { return ode_dimension; }    // read state in from an iterator (and refresh drivers/rates)   template <typename Iterator>   Iterator set_ode_state(Iterator it, double time_);    // write the current state out to an iterator   template <typename Iterator>   Iterator ode_state(Iterator it) const;    // write the current rates (dy/dt) out to an iterator   template <typename Iterator>   Iterator ode_rates(Iterator it) const; };

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: External forcing enters drivers::Drivers object. system holds reference queries current value step: R side, Drivers fits smooth cubic-spline interpolation time series, system can evaluated time adaptive stepper lands . Drivers object can hold several variables accepts time grid covers simulation.
code: void initialize_drivers(const drivers::Drivers &drv);   // attach drivers void set_drivers();                                     // query at current time

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: Compiling model interface requires odelia headers include path. Loading odelia exposes compiled symbols (including XAD runtime) globally via package .onLoad, temporary shared object Rcpp::sourceCpp() builds can resolve — manual odelia_load_dll() call needed.
code: library(odelia)  ex_dir <- system.file("examples/leaf_thermal", package = "odelia")  # Put the odelia headers on the include path for sourceCpp Sys.setenv(PKG_CPPFLAGS = paste0("-I", system.file("include", package = "odelia")))  # Compile the model-specific interface, then load the R6 wrappers Rcpp::sourceCpp(file.path(ex_dir, "src", "leaf_thermal_interface.cpp"), verbose = FALSE) source(file.path(ex_dir, "R", "leaf_thermal_interface.R"))

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: air-temperature driver sinusoidal daily cycle two days.  leaf temperature tracks air temperature buffered transpirational cooling — exactly behaviour expect.
code: # A daily temperature cycle p <- list(Tmean = 32, Tamp = 6, tpeak = 15) time_driver <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.25) t_air <- p$Tmean + p$Tamp * sin(2 * pi * (time_driver - p$tpeak) / 24)  drivers <- Drivers$new() drivers$set_variable("temperature", time_driver, t_air)  # Build the system with its parameters and drivers pars <- LeafThermalSystemPars() lz <- LeafThermalSystem$new(pars, drivers) lz$set_state(c(25), 0) # initial leaf temperature, initial time  # Solve ctrl <- OdeControl$new() runner <- LeafThermalSolver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr, drivers$ptr) times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.5) runner$advance_adaptive(times)  out <- runner$history() out$time <- times head(out) #> # A tibble: 6 × 5 #>    time  T_LC T_air dT_LC   S_tr #>   <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> #> 1   0    25    36.2 5.55  0.0759 #> 2   0.5  27.4  35.7 3.94  0.211  #> 3   1    29.0  35   2.63  0.376  #> 4   1.5  30.0  34.3 1.62  0.505  #> 5   2    30.7  33.6 0.869 0.581  #> 6   2.5  30.9  32.8 0.306 0.615 plot(out$time, out$T_air,   type = "l", col = "grey50", lwd = 2,   xlab = "time (h)", ylab = "temperature (°C)",   ylim = range(out$T_air, out$T_LC) ) lines(out$time, out$T_LC, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2) legend("topright",   legend = c("air", "leaf"),   col = c("grey50", "firebrick"), lwd = 2, bty = "n" )

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: building running system cheap, comparing parameterisations just loop. double maximum transpiration conductance re-run:  Higher conductance means stronger transpirational cooling, leaf stays closer — — air temperature hottest part day.
code: run_strategy <- function(g_tr_max) {   pars <- LeafThermalSystemPars()   pars$g_tr_max <- g_tr_max   lz <- LeafThermalSystem$new(pars, drivers)   lz$set_state(c(25), 0)   runner <- LeafThermalSolver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr, drivers$ptr)   runner$advance_adaptive(times)   runner$history()$T_LC }  base_g <- LeafThermalSystemPars()$g_tr_max T_baseline <- run_strategy(base_g) T_high_cond <- run_strategy(2 * base_g)  plot(times, out$T_air,   type = "l", col = "grey70", lwd = 2,   xlab = "time (h)", ylab = "leaf temperature (°C)",   ylim = range(out$T_air, T_baseline, T_high_cond) ) lines(times, T_baseline, col = "firebrick", lwd = 2) lines(times, T_high_cond, col = "steelblue", lwd = 2) legend("topright",   legend = c("air", "baseline", "2x conductance"),   col = c("grey70", "firebrick", "steelblue"), lwd = 2, bty = "n" )

title: Building your own model with external drivers
text: full, copy-able sources system.file("examples/leaf_thermal", package = "odelia") — start building model. calibrate model’s parameters data using exact gradients, see parameter-fitting vignette.

title: Getting started with odelia
text: simulation odelia assembled pieces: system — ODE model (parameters, state, rule rates dy/dt); control object (OdeControl) — solver’s tolerances step sizes; solver (runner) — drives adaptive RK4-5 stepper forward times request collects solution history.

title: Getting started with odelia
text: Lorenz system three state variables three parameters (sigma, R, b). create set initial state. Lorenz system autonomous (rates depend time directly), time required.
code: lz <- LorenzSystem$new(sigma = 10, R = 28, b = 8 / 3)  # Inspect stored parameters lz$pars() #> [1] 10.000000 28.000000  2.666667  # Set the initial state (x, y, z) lz$set_state(c(1, 1, 1))  # We can query the current state and rates directly lz$state() #> [1] 1 1 1 lz$rates() #> [1]  0.000000 26.000000 -1.666667

title: Getting started with odelia
text: Create control object (defaults sensible) solver, advance adaptive time stepping times want output . history() returns tibble one row per requested output time. stepper adaptive, solver may take internal steps output rows — internal evaluation times available via runner$times().

title: Getting started with odelia
code: plot(out$x, out$z,   type = "l",   xlab = "x", ylab = "z",   main = "Lorenz attractor (via odelia)" )

title: Getting started with odelia
text: OdeControl exposes solver’s tuning knobs — absolute/relative tolerances, state derivative scaling, minimum/maximum/initial step sizes. can inspect current settings tighten :
code: ctrl$get_controls() #> $tol_abs #> [1] 1e-08 #>  #> $tol_rel #> [1] 1e-08 #>  #> $a_y #> [1] 1 #>  #> $a_dydt #> [1] 0 #>  #> $step_size_min #> [1] 1e-08 #>  #> $step_size_max #> [1] 10 #>  #> $step_size_initial #> [1] 1e-06  # Tighten tolerances for a more accurate (but slower) solve ctrl$set_tol_abs(1e-8) ctrl$set_tol_rel(1e-8)

title: Getting started with odelia
text: models external time-varying forcing, define ODE system C++, see Building model external drivers. recover parameters data using exact gradients, see vignette("parameter-fitting", package = "odelia").

title: Parameter fitting with automatic differentiation
text: First solve system known “true” parameters produce data later try recover.
code: true_pars <- c(sigma = 10.0, R = 28.0, b = 8.0 / 3.0)  lz <- LorenzSystem$new(sigma = true_pars[1], R = true_pars[2], b = true_pars[3]) lz$set_initial_state(c(1, 1, 1), t0 = 0)  ctrl <- OdeControl$new()  runner <- Lorenz_Solver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr) runner$advance_adaptive(c(0, 1))  times <- runner$times() hist <- runner$history()  # The internal solver times that coincide with stored output rows obs_index <- which(times %in% hist$time) target_vals <- as.matrix(hist[, c("x", "y", "z")])

title: Parameter fitting with automatic differentiation
text: move system parameters away truth (starting guess), build solver active = TRUE enable AD, register calibration target. $fit() method solves system given parameter vector returns loss exact gradient:
code: initial_guess <- c(sigma = 12.0, R = 30.0, b = 3.0) lz$set_params(initial_guess)  ad_runner <- Lorenz_Solver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr, active = TRUE) ad_runner$set_target(times, target_vals, obs_index) res0 <- ad_runner$fit(params = initial_guess) res0$loss #> [1] 3.480593 res0$gradient #> [1] 1.0865743 0.4861987 1.9423226

title: Parameter fitting with automatic differentiation
text: $fit() call returns loss gradient, cache recent result optimiser re-run solver obtain gradient separately.
code: memoise_fit <- function(runner) {   cache_p <- NULL   cache_res <- NULL    fn <- function(p) {     cache_res <<- runner$fit(params = p)     cache_p <<- p     cache_res$loss   }   gr <- function(p) {     if (!identical(p, cache_p)) fn(p) # populate cache if stale     cache_res$gradient   }   list(obj = fn, grad = gr) }  helper <- memoise_fit(ad_runner)  res <- optim(   par = initial_guess,   fn = helper$obj,   gr = helper$grad,   method = "L-BFGS-B",   control = list(maxit = 100) )  res$convergence # 0 indicates successful convergence #> [1] 0 res$value       # final loss, should be ~0 #> [1] 2.697385e-13

title: Parameter fitting with automatic differentiation
text: optimiser recovers true parameters high precision, confirming gradients produced AD correct usable calibration.
code: rbind(true = true_pars, recovered = res$par) #>           sigma  R        b #> true         10 28 2.666667 #> recovered    10 28 2.666667

title: Authors and Citation
text: Daniel Falster. Author, maintainer. Richard FitzJohn. Author. Andrew O'Reilly-Nugent. Author.

title: Authors and Citation
text: Falster D, FitzJohn R, O'Reilly-Nugent (2026). odelia: ODE Solver Automatic Differentiation, C++ Header Files. R package version 0.1.0, https://github.com/traitecoevo/odelia.
code: @Manual{,   title = {odelia: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files},   author = {Daniel Falster and Richard FitzJohn and Andrew O'Reilly-Nugent},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://github.com/traitecoevo/odelia}, }

what: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: odelia ODE solver implemented C++ header files, using adaptive-step Runge-Kutta 4-5 method, interface R via Rcpp. solver runs entirely compiled code, fast, ODE systems can templated scalar type support automatic differentiation (AD) — letting compute exact gradients solution respect parameters use optimisation calibration. core solver first developed Rich FitzJohn part plant package. package spins code can used widely.

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: Adaptive-step RK4-5 integrator running entirely C++ (~30-90x faster equivalent solved via deSolve; see Lorenz example). Automatic differentiation ODE solutions w.r.t. parameters initial conditions, via vendored XAD library — enabling gradient-based parameter fitting. External drivers: time-varying forcing variables, smoothly interpolated cubic splines queried system step. Header-C++ core Rcpp packages can link . Friendly R6 wrappers around C++ objects.

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: odelia compiles C++ source, need working C++ toolchain (Rtools Windows, Xcode command-line tools macOS, recent g++/clang Linux). :

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: odelia ships one ready--run system, classic Lorenz system, can try solver box. bundled purely demonstration — model problem write small C++ system class (thin Rcpp/R6 interface) solver drives. See Building model complete worked example. example solves bundled Lorenz system: , see vignettes (also rendered package website): vignette("odelia") — getting started core abstractions (source). vignette("parameter-fitting") — recovering parameters automatic differentiation (source). worked examples: Building model external drivers — leaf-thermal model showing define ODE system C++ drive time-varying forcing (source). Lorenz system — also includes speed comparison deSolve.
code: library(odelia)  # Define the system (parameters) and its initial state lz <- LorenzSystem$new(sigma = 10, R = 28, b = 8 / 3) lz$set_state(c(1, 1, 1))   # autonomous system, so no time needed  # Solver control settings (tolerances, step sizes) use sensible defaults ctrl <- OdeControl$new()  # Build a solver (a "runner") and advance with adaptive stepping runner <- Lorenz_Solver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr) runner$advance_adaptive(seq(0, 100, by = 0.01))  # Collect output as a tibble out <- runner$history()

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: solver can templated AD scalar type, can recover exact gradients loss (mismatch solution set target observations) respect system parameters, hand gradient-based optimiser optim(): complete optimisation workflow (recovering known Lorenz parameters) walked vignette("parameter-fitting").
code: # An AD-enabled runner exposes a $fit() method returning loss and gradient ad_runner <- Lorenz_Solver$new(lz$ptr, ctrl$ptr, active = TRUE) ad_runner$set_target(times, target_vals, obs_index)  res <- ad_runner$fit(params = c(sigma = 12, R = 30, b = 3)) res$loss      # scalar mismatch with the target trajectory res$gradient  # exact gradient w.r.t. each parameter

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: odelia organised around core abstractions: System — ODE model. C++ class holds parameters state knows compute rates (right-hand side) dy/dt. Templated scalar type works double AD types. Stepper — numerical integration scheme (adaptive RK4-5) takes one step system, estimating error choose next step size. Solver / runner — drives stepper forward requested set times, applying step-size control collecting solution history. Drivers — external, time-varying forcing variables (e.g. air temperature) system queries integration. Supplied time series interpolated cubic splines. Control (OdeControl) — solver’s tuning knobs: absolute relative tolerances, state/derivative scaling, minimum/maximum/initial step sizes.

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: package released GNU Affero General Public License v3 (AGPL-3). vendored XAD library retains license; see inst/include/XAD/LICENSE.md.

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: Contributions welcome. submitting pull request code repository, agree terms Contributor License Agreement.

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files
text: ODE system odelia consists : system header (inst/include/odelia/examples/*.hpp) — C++ class defining ODE. Must implement ode_size(), set_ode_state(), ode_state(), ode_rates(). Template scalar type AD support. Rcpp interface (src/*_interface.cpp) — [[Rcpp::export]] functions exposing system R. R wrapper (R/*-interface.R, optional) — R6 classes providing friendlier API around external pointers. demo script (examples/*/demo.R) — Runnable demonstration. ### Testing variants Use Makefile targets depending level test coverage want. Runs package tests local compile. Fast default day--day work. Runs testthat::test_local() source tree. Installs tests runs testthat::test_package() installed package copy.
code: make test make test-local make test-installed

title: ODE Solver with Automatic Differentiation, in C++ Header Files — odelia-package
text: odelia provides ODE solver C++ header files, using     adaptive-step Runge-Kutta 4-5 method, interface R via Rcpp.     ODE systems can templated scalar type support automatic     differentiation, enabling exact gradients parameter fitting.

text: Odelia new package, arsising https://github.com/traitecoevo/plant/. project, Rich FitzJohn built custom ODE solver, using Runge-Kutta4-5 method, C++. ’m spinning code package, want use elsewhere. odelia now loads shared library global symbol visibility .onLoad, packages LinkingTo: odelia instantiate Solver can resolve compiled XAD runtime symbols load time without per-package linker hacks (#26).
